Article.ArticleTitle: Dissolved organic matter enhances sunlight-induced transformation of tire rubber antioxidant 6PPD to 6PPD-quinone in aquatic environments.

Abstract.AbstractText: The tire antioxidant N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (6PPD) is ubiquitously distributed in aquatic environments owing to its extensive application. Its phototransformation under sunlight represents a critical pathway for producing the highly toxic derivative 6PPD-quinone (6PPD-Q). However, the role of dissolved organic matter (DOM), a key constituent of natural waters, in mediating 6PPD's photoconversion and the underlying mechanisms remains unexplored. This study systematically investigated the comprehensive effects of Suwannee River DOM (SRDOM) on 6PPD phototransformation. Our findings revealed that SRDOM significantly promoted the photodegradation of 6PPD, with the observed rate constant (kobs) increasing by 2-14 times compared to that in pure water. Notably, SRDOM substantially enhanced 6PPD-Q accumulation, with the maximum concentration reaching 9.40 µg/L. Multifaceted experiments demonstrated that SRDOM photosensitized the generation of multiple reactive species, including the excited triplet state (3DOM*), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (O2•-), and singlet oxygen (1O2). Among them, 1O2 and O2•- were identified as the primary species driving 6PPD-Q formation, whereas •OH and 3DOM* dominated its further degradation. Density functional theory calculations indicated that the aniline moieties of 6PPD were susceptible to both electrophilic and nucleophilic attacks, supporting the proposed transformation pathways. Furthermore, FT-ICR MS analysis suggested that SRDOM participated in 6PPD phototransformation mainly through oxygen addition (+O) and decarbonylation (-CO) reactions. Nitrogen-rich components (e.g., tryptophan) in SRDOM were pinpointed as key facilitators for 6PPD-Q formation by enhancing 1O2 production. This study elucidates a crucial enhancement pathway for 6PPD-Q formation in sunlit waters, providing novel insights into the environmental fate and risks of 6PPD.
